Replaced Google Fonts `<link>` tag (from `_document.js`) with `next/font/google` Inter import in `app/layout.tsx`. The Inter font is loaded via `Inter({ subsets: ['latin'], weight: ['400', '500', '600'], display: 'swap' })` and applied to `<html>` via `className={inter.className}`.

Why: `next/font` self-hosts the font file, eliminating the external network request and reducing layout shift (CLS). The font is downloaded at build time and served from the same origin. The display:swap option is the new approach since Next.js 13+ handles font-display swap by default with next/font.

input.content:
Unified `_app.js` (layout + AppProvider wrapping + global CSS import) and `_document.js` (HTML shell, Google Fonts links, favicon, meta description) into a single `app/layout.tsx` with the Metadata API. The `<Html>`, `<Head>`, `<Main>`, `<NextScript>` pattern from `_document.js` is gone — replaced by a standard `<html>` element, `export const metadata` object, and `next/font`. Metadata (title template, description, favicon) is declared via the Metadata API export.

Why: App Router eliminates the separate `_document.js` concept. The root layout handles all HTML shell concerns, and the Metadata API provides a declarative, type-safe way to set `<head>` content without custom components. Title templates (`%s - My Blog`) enable per-page titles via `generateMetadata()`.

Converted all source files from JavaScript (`.js`) to TypeScript (`.tsx`/`.ts`). Created a shared `types/index.ts` with `Post`, `Comment`, and `ApiPost` interfaces used across pages and API route handlers. The conversion happened alongside the App Router migration so all new route files were written as `.tsx` from the start.

Why: TypeScript provides type safety for API responses, component props, and data fetching. Shared type definitions prevent mismatches between page components and API routes (e.g., Post interface used by both the blog page (JSONPlaceholder fetch) and the mock API handlers). Strict mode in tsconfig.json catches null-reference errors and implicit-any issues.

input.content:
Added `loading.tsx` files at three levels: `/app/loading.tsx` (global fallback), `/app/blog/loading.tsx` (blog listing skeleton with placeholder post cards), and `/app/blog/[id]/loading.tsx` (simple loading text for individual posts). These replace the imperative `router.isFallback` check that was used in the Pages Router `pages/blog/[id].js`.

Why: App Router automatically wraps page components in React Suspense boundaries when a `loading.tsx` file exists in the same segment. This provides instant loading UI without manual conditional rendering or state management. The skeleton loading pattern in blog/loading.tsx shows placeholder cards matching the real grid layout for a smoother UX than a spinner.

input.content:
Replaced all `useRouter().push()` and `useRouter().back()` calls with `<Link>` components from `next/link`. The home page "View All Posts" button now wraps `<Link href="/blog">`, blog "Read More" buttons use `<Link href="/blog/${post.id}">`, the 404 "Go Back Home" uses `<Link href="/">`, and the blog post "← Back" button uses `<Link href="/blog">` instead of `router.back()`. This eliminated the need for client components or `'use client'` directives on any page — all pages remain server components.

Why: `<Link>` is the preferred navigation primitive in App Router. It enables prefetching (automatic for static routes, opt-in for dynamic) and works in server components without a `'use client'` directive. Using `router.back()` would have required extracting a client component, adding unnecessary client-side JavaScript. The back-to-blog-index pattern (Link to /blog) is also more deterministic than browser history back.
